section: Co-precipitation mechanism of Cu-rich phase and κ-carbide precipitates in Fe-28Mn-10Al-1C-3Cu austenitic low-density steel

context_update.summary: This chunk introduces the materials (Fe-28Mn-10Al-1C and Fe-28Mn-10Al-1C-3Cu) and processing route (solution treatment at 1000 °C 1 h followed by aging at 550 °C 3 h). It reports TEM/EDS/HRTEM characterization of aged 3Cu steel, revealing that κ-carbide and Cu-rich phase are coherent with the austenite matrix with cube-on-cube orientation relationship. Cu-rich phase appears as irregular ovals (~10 nm) and bars (~30 nm), with an FCC structure (a=0.3652 nm) and (002) d-spacing 0.182 nm, while κ-carbide has a=0.3775 nm. Lattice misfit calculations show low misfits (1.0 % for Cu-rich/austenite, 2.3 % for κ-carbide/austenite, 3.4 % between them). Cu-rich phase nucleates in austenite due to lower energy barrier and its precipitation is promoted by prior κ-carbide precipitation. EDS mapping suggests Cu is expelled from κ-carbide and enriches in adjacent matrix, eventually forming Cu-rich clusters. The critical austenite lattice parameter for κ-carbide precipitation is ~0.3670 nm; measured values (0.3687 nm for 0Cu, 0.3689 nm for 3Cu) exceed this, and Cu addition raises the parameter favoring precipitation. Figures: Fig.1 SAED, Fig.2 TEM-EDS-HRTEM.
key_variables: lattice parameter of austenite (0.3687 nm, 0.3689 nm)
lattice parameter of Cu-rich phase (0.3652 nm)
microstructure: Cu-rich phase (irregular oval ~10 nm, bars ~30 nm)
no dislocations or stacking faults in Cu-rich phase
mechanisms: Cu migration and enrichment near κ-carbide
Cu cluster formation and Cu-rich phase precipitation
nucleation energy barrier based on lattice misfit
critical lattice parameter for κ-carbide precipitation
figures_or_tables: Figure 2 (TEM, EDS mapping, HRTEM, IFFT images)
unresolved_terms: composition boundary formula for intragranular κ-carbide precipitation (not given in this chunk)

rolling_context_summary:
Summary: This chunk introduces the materials (Fe-28Mn-10Al-1C and Fe-28Mn-10Al-1C-3Cu) and processing route (solution treatment at 1000 °C 1 h followed by aging at 550 °C 3 h). It reports TEM/EDS/HRTEM characterization of aged 3Cu steel, revealing that κ-carbide and Cu-rich phase are coherent with the austenite matrix with cube-on-cube orientation relationship. Cu-rich phase appears as irregular ovals (~10 nm) and bars (~30 nm), with an FCC structure (a=0.3652 nm) and (002) d-spacing 0.182 nm, while κ-carbide has a=0.3775 nm. Lattice misfit calculations show low misfits (1.0 % for Cu-rich/austenite, 2.3 % for κ-carbide/austenite, 3.4 % between them). Cu-rich phase nucleates in austenite due to lower energy barrier and its precipitation is promoted by prior κ-carbide precipitation. EDS mapping suggests Cu is expelled from κ-carbide and enriches in adjacent matrix, eventually forming Cu-rich clusters. The critical austenite lattice parameter for κ-carbide precipitation is ~0.3670 nm; measured values (0.3687 nm for 0Cu, 0.3689 nm for 3Cu) exceed this, and Cu addition raises the parameter favoring precipitation. Figures: Fig.1 SAED, Fig.2 TEM-EDS-HRTEM.
Material systems: Fe-28Mn-10Al-1C (0Cu steel); Fe-28Mn-10Al-1C-3Cu (3Cu steel); κ-carbide; Cu-rich phase; austenitic matrix
Sample aliases: 0Cu steel; 3Cu steel; aged 3Cu steel
Processing route: solution treatment at 1000 °C for 1 h; aging at 550 °C for 3 h; Cu addition (3 wt%)
Key variables: Cu content (0 vs 3 wt%); solution treatment temperature (1000 °C); aging temperature (550 °C); aging time (3 h); lattice parameter of austenite (0.3687 nm, 0.3689 nm); lattice parameter of κ-carbide (0.3775 nm); lattice parameter of Cu-rich phase (0.3652 nm); critical lattice parameter (0.3670 nm)
Microstructure: κ-carbide precipitates (dots or bars); Cu-rich phase (irregular oval ~10 nm, bars ~30 nm); coherent interfaces with austenite; no dislocations or stacking faults in Cu-rich phase; cube-on-cube orientation relationship ([11 0]γ//[11 0]κ//[110]Cu, (11 1)γ//(11 1)κ//(11 1)Cu)
Mechanisms: κ-carbide preferential precipitation; Cu migration and enrichment near κ-carbide; Cu cluster formation and Cu-rich phase precipitation; nucleation energy barrier based on lattice misfit; critical lattice parameter for κ-carbide precipitation
Figures or tables: Figure 1 (SAED patterns); Figure 2 (TEM, EDS mapping, HRTEM, IFFT images)
Unresolved terms: composition boundary formula for intragranular κ-carbide precipitation (not given in this chunk)
